cards.front: How should a work email/report open?
cards.back: Lead with the conclusion or your ask, then reasons. Readers are busy — point first (BLUF).

cards.front: How to give feedback that lands?
cards.back: Use SBI: Situation + Behavior + Impact — facts and effect, not character.
It names the actor and is shorter: "we shipped it" > "it was shipped".

cards.front: One quick way to tighten prose?
cards.back: Cut filler: "in order to"→"to", "due to the fact that"→"because".

cards.front: What makes a good email subject?
cards.back: Specific + actionable: "Need sign-off on X by Fri", not "Quick question".
Ideally one; multiple get half-answered. Split them or use a numbered list.

cards.front: How to say no politely at work?
cards.back: Acknowledge, decline clearly, offer an alternative or trade-off.
Decisions, owners, and dated next steps — not a transcript.
Read it aloud — your ear catches clunky phrasing and missing words.

cards.front: Long message — help the reader how?
cards.back: Put a one-line TL;DR at the top with the point and any action.
Answer first, then group supporting points beneath it — top-down, not a slow build-up.
Specifics persuade: "cut load time from 8s to 2s" beats "improved performance".
"decide" beats "make a decision" — verb+noun padding weakens and lengthens.
Keep list items the same grammatical form — all verbs or all nouns — for readability.

cards.front: What makes a clear call to action?
cards.back: State exactly what to do and by when: "Reply with approval by Thu 5pm".
Short paragraphs, bullets, and bold key phrases — most people skim first.
Drop or define jargon; assume one less expert reader than you think.
After each point, ask "so what?"; if there's no answer, cut it.
One idea per sentence is easier to follow; break long ones at the "and"/"but".
Say what to do, not just what to avoid — positive instructions are clearer.

cards.front: How to set the right tone?
cards.back: Match formality to reader and channel; chat ≠ a client email.
No — draft fast, then edit separately. Mixing both stalls you.

cards.front: Where to put the key word in a heading?
cards.back: Up front: "Refund policy — 30 days" beats "Our policy on refunds".

cards.front: Vague claim — how to fix it?
cards.back: Add a number or example: "saves time" → "saves ~2 hours a week".
